perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the Sponsorship Rights granted to Hydron hereunder: (a) Hydron shall pay to the Dolphins an aggregate amount of $96,000 (plus any applicable sales and other taxes) as follows: Hydron shall pay Dolphins the sum of $24,000 (plus any applicable sales and other taxes) on July 1, 1997, July 1, 1998, July 1, 1999 and July 1, 2000.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the Sponsorship Rights granted to Hydron hereunder: (a) Hydron shall pay to the Dolphins an aggregate amount of $96,000 (plus any applicable sales and other taxes) as follows: Hydron shall pay Dolphins the sum of $24,000 (plus any applicable sales and other taxes) on July 1, 1997, July 1, 1998, July 1, 1999 and July 1, 2000. Payments may be offset unilaterally by Hydron for perceived losses, at Hydron's sole discretion, with written notice to Dolphins.
perturbation.explanation: The added clause gives Hydron the right to offset payments for perceived losses, introducing ambiguity and potentially violating contract law regarding agreed-upon payment terms. This contradicts the initial agreement of a fixed payment schedule.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 672.717 - Deduction of damages from the price.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes 672.717 outlines the conditions under which a buyer can deduct damages from the price due under a contract. The modification allows Hydron to unilaterally offset payments for 'perceived losses' without defining 'perceived losses' or specifying a dispute resolution mechanism, which deviates from the statute's requirement for a breach as a basis for deduction and creates a legally dubious self-help remedy.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 672 > Section 717 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 672 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: SALES Entire Chapter SECTION 717 Deduction of damages from the price. F.S. 672.717 672.717 Deduction of damages from the price. — The buyer on notifying the seller of her or his intention to do so may deduct all or any part of the damages resulting from any breach of the contract from any part of the price still due under the same contract. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254; s. 611, ch. 97-102. Note. — s. 2-717, U.C.C.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a clause allowing Hydron to unilaterally offset payments for 'perceived losses,' which directly contradicts the established payment schedule. Florida Statute 672.717, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows a buyer to deduct damages from the price only 'resulting from any breach of the contract.' The modification allows Hydron to deduct for 'perceived losses' without a defined breach or dispute resolution mechanism, thus creating a contradiction with the statute's requirement.

perturbation.original_text: The Dolphins will provide to Hydron: o One full page, four (4) color advertisement for Hydron in all ten (10) issues of GameDay Magazine, the official publication of the National Football League and the Miami Dolphins distributed at Pro Player Stadium during each year during the Term.
perturbation.changed_text: The Dolphins will provide to Hydron: o One full page, four (4) color advertisement for Hydron in all ten (10) issues of GameDay Magazine, which may or may not be distributed at Pro Player Stadium, at the Dolphins' discretion, during each year during the Term.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'which may or may not be distributed at Pro Player Stadium, at the Dolphins' discretion', the contract now allows the Dolphins to potentially not fulfill a crucial part of the advertising benefit, weakening the agreement's certainty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 672.306 - Output, requirements and exclusive dealings.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 672.306(1) implies good faith in exclusive dealing contracts. While not strictly an exclusive dealing, the provision for advertising in a game day magazine suggests a reasonable expectation that it will be distributed at the stadium. Allowing the Dolphins discretion to withhold distribution undermines this expectation and potentially breaches the implied covenant of good faith, making the agreed-upon performance uncertain.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 672 > Section 306 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 672 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: SALES Entire Chapter SECTION 306 Output, requirements, and exclusive dealings. F.S. 672.306 672.306 Output, requirements, and exclusive dealings. — (1) A term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith, except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. (2) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254. Note. — s. 2-306, U.C.C.
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the principle of good faith as implied by Florida Statute 672.306(1). The original contract specified distribution at Pro Player Stadium, creating a reasonable expectation for Hydron. The modification, allowing the Dolphins discretion to withhold distribution, undermines this expectation. While 672.306(1) primarily addresses output and requirement contracts, the Law Explanation correctly extends the principle of good faith to the agreement, as it suggests a reasonable expectation. This discretion introduces uncertainty, creating a potential breach of implied covenant of good faith, especially given the original contract’s clear statement of distribution at Pro Player Stadium.

perturbation.original_text: In connection with any action arising from or in connection with the enforcement of this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to an award of its expenses, including reasonable attorneys' fees and disbursements, incurred or paid before and at trial or any other proceeding which may be instituted, at any tribunal level, and whether or not suit or any other proceeding is instituted.
perturbation.changed_text: In connection with any action arising from or in connection with the enforcement of this Agreement, attorneys' fees shall be awarded at the sole discretion of the presiding judge regardless of the prevailing party.
perturbation.explanation: The change makes the award of attorney's fees discretionary, contradicting the principle of awarding fees to the prevailing party in contract disputes, creating uncertainty about cost recovery.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 57.105 - Attorney fees and other costs.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 57.105 addresses attorney's fees and costs, often awarded to the prevailing party in litigation. While it doesn't mandate this in every case, the original clause created a contractual right to such fees. The modified clause removes this right and gives the judge complete discretion, deviating from the standard contractual expectation and introducing uncertainty that could discourage legitimate claims or defenses based on fear of unrecoverable legal costs, even if successful.

View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Florida Statutes Title VI - Civil Practice and Procedure Chapter 57 - Court Costs 57.105 - Attorney’s Fee; Sanctions for Raising Unsupported Claims or Defenses; Exceptions; Service of Motions; Damages for Delay of Litigation. Universal Citation: FL Stat § 57.105 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 57.105 Attorney’s fee; sanctions for raising unsupported claims or defenses; exceptions; service of motions; damages for delay of litigation.— (1) Upon the court’s initiative or motion of any party, the court shall award a reasonable attorney’s fee, including prejudgment interest, to be paid to the prevailing party in equal amounts by the losing party and the losing party’s attorney on any claim or defense at any time during a civil proceeding or action in which the court finds that the losing party or the losing party’s attorney knew or should have known that a claim or defense when initially presented to the court or at any time before trial: (a) Was not supported by the material facts necessary to establish the claim or defense; or (b) Would not be supported by the application of then-existing law to those material facts. (2) At any time in any civil proceeding or action in which the moving party proves by a preponderance of the evidence that any action taken by the opposing party, including, but not limited to, the filing of any pleading or part thereof, the assertion of or response to any discovery demand, the assertion of any claim or defense, or the r
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original agreement by making attorney's fees discretionary rather than awarded to the prevailing party. This misalignment qualifies as a 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' because the altered contractual language deviates from a standard contractual expectation. Florida Statute 57.105, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, discusses attorney's fees and costs often awarded to the prevailing party. While the statute itself doesn't mandate this in every case, the original contract created a specific right to such fees. The changed text removes this right, contradicting the original intent and introducing uncertainty about cost recovery, thus creating a legal contradiction as the contract now uses terms which contradict a commonly expected legal outcome for the prevailing party.
